properties.trigger: no Me entity (person with is_self: true) exists at session start

data.short_description: New-user onboarding workflow. Runs once, on the first session where no 'Me' entity exists: explains Orbismo, interviews the user, and builds the initial graph (Me, home, key people, current projects). Read the lore chunk and follow it exactly; when onboarding completes, set this rule's status to 'archived'.

lore.title: Workflow: New User Onboarding
lore.content:
TRIGGER: First Session found no person with is_self: true. Run this once, then archive this rule (see COMPLETION).

Briefly explain what Orbismo is (a living map of their life — people, places, memories, connections — they just talk, you handle the rest), then ask these questions ONE AT A TIME, writing each answer to the graph as you go:
1. What's your name? → create the "Me" person entity with is_self: true and person_type: "human". is_self: true is the one non-negotiable write of onboarding — without it, every future session fails to find "Me" and re-runs onboarding.
2. Where do you live? → create the home as a place and link Me LIVES_AT it.
3. Who are the most important people in your life right now? → create each person and link with the right family/social edge (SPOUSE_OF, PARENT_OF, FRIENDS_WITH, ...).
4. Anything significant going on — a job, project, transition, something you're looking forward to? → a project (Me WORKS_ON it) or an upcoming dated event.
5. Anything you especially want to remember or keep track of? → these answers are usually lore-shaped; attach lore to the most specific relevant entity per the world's journaling rules.

Build as you go — don't wait until question 5 to start creating entities. Close by reflecting back what you learned and mentioning what this world can already do: "So you're in Austin, married to Sarah, and in the middle of a kitchen reno — good start. I can also track movies you watch and books you read — just mention them."

COMPLETION (required): once "Me" exists and the questions are done, set this rule's status to "archived" via update_entity — onboarding must not trigger again. If the user bails mid-onboarding, leave this rule active; the next session resumes naturally (search before creating — partial data may already exist).

properties.trigger: user mentions watching a movie or film, their watchlist, movie ratings or reviews, rewatching, or going to the cinema/theater

data.short_description: Movie Tracker mini-app — a personal movie diary. Tracks movies as items, watch sessions as events with per-watch ratings, watchlists, favorites, and co-watcher history. Full operating rules are in this entity's lore chunks: Data Model, Log a Watch, Watchlist/Favorites/Edits, Queries & Stats. Read all lore before acting on movie-related requests.

lore.title: Data Model
lore.content:
MOVIE = item entity. Name: the movie's title (slugs derive from names; add the year to the name only when two tracked movies share a title). Properties: item_type: "movie", year (number), director, genres (array), runtime_minutes (number), status ("watchlist" | "watched"), favorite (boolean), where_to_stream (optional). Tag every movie item "movie-tracker". Every movie item gets RELATED_TO interest/watching_movies (collection anchor).

WATCH SESSION = event entity. Name: "Watched <title> (<YYYY-MM-DD>)", dated so every watch of the same movie gets its own slug. Properties: event_type: "movie_watch", date (human-readable), date_year/date_month/date_day (numbers), rating (1-10), impression (1-3 sentence review), rewatch (boolean), format ("theater" | "streaming" | "physical"), mood (optional). Tag "movie-tracker".

RELATIONSHIPS per watch: event RELATED_TO item (the movie); each watcher person ATTENDED event (always include the world owner); event OCCURRED_AT place if location known (create the place if new). Optionally link the movie item via REFERENCES to a reference entity (e.g. an IMDb page) when a URL is provided.

lore.title: Workflow: Log a Watch
lore.content:
WORKFLOW: Log a watch ("I watched X last night with Y").
1. Search entities for the movie item. If missing, create it per the Data Model, filling year/director/genres from general knowledge; set status: "watched".
2. If the movie existed with status "watchlist", update status to "watched".
3. Create the watch event. Ask for a 1-10 rating and a one-line impression if not given; rating is the single most important field — always try to capture it. Set rewatch: true if a prior watch event for this movie exists.
4. Create relationships: event RELATED_TO movie; ATTENDED edges for the owner and each named co-watcher (create person entities for unknown co-watchers after confirming with the user); OCCURRED_AT if a place was mentioned.
5. Confirm back with a one-line summary: title, rating, who, where.

lore.title: Workflow: Watchlist, Favorites, Edits
lore.content:
WORKFLOW: Watchlist ("add X to my watchlist"). Create the movie item with status: "watchlist", tag "movie-tracker", RELATED_TO interest/watching_movies. No event is created until it's actually watched. To list the watchlist: search items tagged "movie-tracker" with status "watchlist".

WORKFLOW: Favorites. "X is one of my favorites" → set favorite: true on the movie item. Favorites list = movie items with favorite: true.

WORKFLOW: Re-rate / edit review. Update the rating/impression on the most recent watch event for that movie, not on the movie item. The movie item never stores a rating; ratings live on watch events so rewatches can differ — each watch gets its own rating.

lore.title: Workflow: Queries & Stats
lore.content:
WORKFLOW: Queries & stats.
- "What did I watch with <person>?" → query_relationships: that person's ATTENDED edges to events with event_type movie_watch, then follow RELATED_TO to movie items.
- "What have I rated highest?" → list movie_watch events, sort by rating property.
- "Have I seen X?" → search for the movie item; check status and any linked watch events.
- "What should I watch?" → list watchlist items; optionally recommend based on genres of high-rated (8+) watches.
- Year in review → filter movie_watch events by date_year; report count, average rating, top co-watcher (most ATTENDED overlaps), most-watched genre.
Presentation: keep answers conversational; a compact list or table only when 4+ titles.

data.short_description: Collection anchor for the Movie Tracker app. All movie items link here via RELATED_TO so the full collection can be queried from one hub.

properties.trigger: user mentions reading, starting, or finishing a book, their reading list or to-read pile, book ratings or reviews, rereading, audiobooks, book series, or asks for book recommendations

data.short_description: Book Tracker mini-app — a personal reading diary. Tracks books as items with shelves (to-read, reading, read, DNF), reading progress, completed read-throughs as events with per-read ratings, favorites, series, first-class author entities (CREATED_BY), and taste-based recommendations. Full operating rules are in this entity's lore chunks: Data Model, Reading Lifecycle, Read List/Favorites/Edits, Queries/Stats/Recommendations. Read all lore before acting on book-related requests.

lore.title: Data Model
lore.content:
BOOK = item entity. Name: the book's title (slugs derive from names; add the author's surname to the name only when two tracked books share a title). Properties: item_type: "book", author, year (number, first publication), genres (array), pages (number), series + series_number (optional), format ("physical" | "ebook" | "audiobook", optional until known), status ("to_read" | "reading" | "read" | "dnf"), favorite (boolean), started_date (ISO date, while reading), current_page OR progress_percent (number, while reading), dnf_reason (optional). Tag every book item "book-tracker". Every book item gets RELATED_TO interest/reading (collection anchor).

READ-THROUGH = event entity, created when a book is FINISHED — not per sitting; a read spans days or weeks, so progress lives on the item and the event marks completion. Name: "Read <title> (<YYYY-MM-DD>)" using the finish date, so every read-through of the same book gets its own slug. Properties: event_type: "book_read", date (human-readable finish date), date_year/date_month/date_day (numbers), rating (1-10), impression (1-3 sentence review), reread (boolean), format, started_date (optional). Tag "book-tracker".

AUTHOR = person entity, created once and reused across books, named with the author's full name. Properties: person_type: "human", occupation: "author". Tags: "author", "book-tracker" — the author tag marks public figures so they stay distinct from personal contacts; never link an author socially to the world owner unless the user actually knows them. Every book item links CREATED_BY to its author person (search for the author before creating, in the same operation as the book). Keep the book's author property as the canonical display spelling; the CREATED_BY edge is the queryable source of truth.

RELATIONSHIPS per read-through: event RELATED_TO item (the book); the reader ATTENDED event (always include the world owner; add co-readers such as read-aloud or book-club partners when named). Optionally link the book item via REFERENCES to a reference entity when a URL is provided.

lore.title: Workflow: Reading Lifecycle
lore.content:
WORKFLOW: Start a book ("I started X").
1. Search for the book item. If missing, create it per the Data Model, filling author/year/genres/pages from general knowledge, and link CREATED_BY to its author person (search first, create if new).
2. Set status: "reading", started_date, and format if known. No event yet.

WORKFLOW: Progress ("I'm on page 120" / "about halfway"). Update current_page or progress_percent on the item. No event.

WORKFLOW: Finish a book ("I finished X").
1. Set status: "read"; clear current_page/progress_percent.
2. Create the read-through event dated by the finish. Ask for a 1-10 rating and a one-line impression if not given; rating is the single most important field — always try to capture it. Set reread: true if a prior read-through event for this book exists.
3. Create relationships: event RELATED_TO book; ATTENDED for the owner and any named co-readers.
4. Confirm back in one line: title, rating, dates.

WORKFLOW: Abandon ("I gave up on X"). Set status: "dnf" and dnf_reason if offered; clear progress. No event and no rating pressure — a DNF is itself useful signal for recommendations.

lore.title: Workflow: Read List, Favorites, Edits
lore.content:
WORKFLOW: Read list ("add X to my reading list"). Create the book item with status: "to_read", tag "book-tracker", RELATED_TO interest/reading, and CREATED_BY its author person (search first, create if new). No event is created until it's actually read. To list the read list: search items tagged "book-tracker" with status "to_read".

WORKFLOW: Favorites. "X is one of my favorite books" → set favorite: true on the book item. Favorites list = book items with favorite: true.

WORKFLOW: Re-rate / edit review. Update the rating/impression on the most recent read-through event for that book, not on the book item. The book item never stores a rating; ratings live on read-through events so rereads can differ — each read gets its own rating.

WORKFLOW: Series. Books in a series share the series property; order by series_number. When the user finishes a series book, offer the next installment for the read list.

lore.title: Workflow: Queries, Stats & Recommendations
lore.content:
WORKFLOW: Queries & stats.
- "What am I reading?" → items with status "reading", with current progress.
- "Have I read X?" → search for the book item; check status and any linked read-through events.
- "What did I think of X?" → the most recent read-through event's rating and impression.
- "What have I rated highest?" → list book_read events, sort by rating property.
- "What have I read by <author>?" / "Have I read everything by X?" → query_relationships (mode: neighbors) on the author person → their CREATED_BY books → check each book's status and read-through events. Never answer author questions by string-matching the author property; the edge is the source of truth.
- Year in review → filter book_read events by date_year; report count, total pages (sum pages of finished books), average rating, top genre and author, rereads. Count DNFs separately — they are not finished books.

WORKFLOW: Recommendations ("what should I read next?").
1. Start from the read list (status "to_read"), ranked by overlap with the genres, authors, and series of high-rated (8+) reads.
2. If the list is thin or the user wants something new, suggest a few titles from general knowledge that fit those tastes — favor authors whose books they rated highly (follow CREATED_BY edges), respect patterns in their DNFs (genres or styles they abandon), and say in one line why each fits.
3. Offer to add accepted suggestions to the read list.
Presentation: keep answers conversational; a compact list or table only when 4+ titles.

data.short_description: Collection anchor for the Book Tracker app. All book items link here via RELATED_TO so the full collection can be queried from one hub.

properties.note: Collection anchor managed by the Movie Tracker app
Collection anchor managed by the Book Tracker app
